title: Hugging Face Releases ML Intern, AI Agent That Beats Claude Code on Scientific Reasoning
summary: Hugging Face launches an open-source AI agent that autonomously reads papers, pulls datasets, and runs GPU training jobs, outperforming Claude Code and Codex on key benchmarks.
content: Hugging Face has released ML Intern, an open source AI agent that autonomously researches, writes and runs machine learning code, with early benchmark results showing it outperforming Anthropic's Claude Code on scientific reasoning and OpenAI's Codex on a healthcare evaluation. The agent pulls papers from arXiv and hf.co/papers, walks citation graphs, reformats datasets, and launches training jobs on Hugging Face Jobs when local GPUs are not available. In one test, ML Intern pushed a Qwen3-1.7B model's GPQA score from 10% to 32% in under 10 hours, surpassing Claude Code's best result of 22.99%; on a healthcare benchmark it generated 1,100 synthetic training examples and beat Codex on HealthBench by 60%. The agent runs an agentic loop of up to 300 iterations per task, with a tool router for Hugging Face docs, datasets, and papers plus GitHub code search and sandboxed execution. Hugging Face is provisioning $1,000 in GPU resources and Anthropic credits for early users, with the CLI and web app both live today.

title: Google Launches Deep Research Max, Autonomous Research Agent Built on Gemini 3.1 Pro
summary: Google DeepMind releases Deep Research and Deep Research Max agents with MCP support, native chart generation, and top scores on HLE, BrowseComp, and DeepSearchQA benchmarks.
content: Google DeepMind has launched two new autonomous research agents, Deep Research and Deep Research Max, in public preview via the Gemini API, both built on Gemini 3.1 Pro. Deep Research Max is designed for asynchronous background workflows such as overnight due diligence reports, running over 160 web queries per task and leveraging extended test-time compute to iteratively reason, search, and refine its output. Both agents now support Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers, enabling them to tap proprietary data sources from financial providers like FactSet, S&P Global, and PitchBook, and can natively generate charts and infographics inline with their reports. Deep Research Max scored 93.3% on DeepSearchQA, 85.9% on BrowseComp, and 54% on Humanity's Last Exam—outperforming GPT-5.4 Thinking and the previous December 2025 release across all three benchmarks. The agents also gain multimodal research grounding, collaborative planning controls, and real-time streaming of intermediate reasoning steps.

title: OpenAI Launches ChatGPT Images 2.0 with Thinking Mode and Full API Access
summary: OpenAI releases Images 2.0, a major upgrade to its image generation model featuring thinking-enabled workflows, dense text rendering, and developer access via the new gpt-image-2 API.
content: OpenAI has launched ChatGPT Images 2.0, a significantly improved image generation model now available across ChatGPT, Codex, and the API under the model ID gpt-image-2. The update delivers major gains in following detailed instructions, rendering dense text, placing objects accurately, and supporting aspect ratios from 3:1 to 1:3—covering banners, slides, posters, and mobile formats. A new Thinking mode—available to Plus, Pro, Business, and Enterprise subscribers—lets the model reason through tasks, search the web for real-time information, and generate up to 8 distinct images from a single prompt. CEO Sam Altman described the leap as equivalent to going from GPT-3 to GPT-5 all at once, highlighting improvements in multilingual support and handling of UI elements, diagrams, and dense layouts. API users can generate images at up to 2K resolution, enabling developers to embed professional-grade image generation directly into products and business workflows.

title: Google Unveils Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform and $750M Partner Fund at Cloud Next 2026
summary: Google rebranded Vertex AI as the Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform at Cloud Next, introducing tools for building, governing, and scaling thousands of AI agents across enterprise organizations.
content: At Google Cloud Next 2026, Google overhauled its enterprise AI strategy by rebranding Vertex AI as the Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform—a unified system for developing, deploying, governing, and monitoring AI agents at scale. The platform is organized around four pillars (build, scale, govern, optimize) and includes Agent Studio for low-code agent creation, a new graph-based Agent Development Kit for orchestrating multi-agent pipelines, and an Agent Runtime capable of sub-second cold starts and multi-day long-running agents. Governance features include Agent Identity (unique cryptographic IDs with authorization policies), Agent Gateway (enforcing security against prompt injection and data leakage), and Agent Anomaly Detection to flag rogue agent behavior before it escalates. Google CEO Sundar Pichai reported that 75% of all Google code is now AI-generated and approved by engineers and that 75% of Google Cloud customers are actively using AI products, with Gemini Enterprise seeing 40% quarter-over-quarter growth in paid monthly active users. The announcements were accompanied by a $750 million fund for Google's partner ecosystem and early-access agreements with McKinsey, Deloitte, Accenture, and Bain & Company.

title: OpenAI Open-Sources Privacy Filter, a State-of-the-Art PII Detection Model
summary: OpenAI releases Privacy Filter under Apache 2.0, a 1.5B-parameter model that detects and redacts personally identifiable information with 97% F1 accuracy and runs entirely on-device.
content: OpenAI has released Privacy Filter, an open-weight model for detecting and redacting personally identifiable information (PII) in text, available under the Apache 2.0 license on Hugging Face and GitHub. The model is a 1.5B-parameter bidirectional token classifier with 50M active parameters that processes up to 128,000 tokens in a single forward pass, covering eight PII categories including persons, addresses, emails, phone numbers, account numbers, and secrets such as API keys and passwords. Unlike traditional rule-based tools, Privacy Filter uses deep language understanding to make context-sensitive decisions—distinguishing public information from private data—and achieves an F1 score of 97.43% on the corrected PII-Masking-300k benchmark. Because the model runs locally, sensitive text never leaves the user's machine, making it well-suited for training pipelines, logging systems, and document review workflows in regulated industries. OpenAI uses a fine-tuned version internally and notes the model can be quickly adapted to domain-specific privacy policies with even small amounts of labeled data.

title: White House Releases National AI Policy Framework, Calls on Congress to Preempt State AI Laws
summary: The Trump administration's National Policy Framework for AI recommends Congress establish a unified federal standard that would override the growing patchwork of state-level AI regulations.
content: On March 20, 2026, the White House released its National Policy Framework for Artificial Intelligence, a comprehensive set of legislative recommendations aimed at establishing a coherent, nationally unified approach to AI governance. The Framework's most consequential provision recommends that Congress preempt state AI laws that 'impose undue burdens,' seeking to replace measures like Colorado's AI Act and California's CCPA automated decision-making amendments with a single federal standard. Carve-outs preserve state authority to enforce child-protection laws, exercise zoning powers over data centers, and regulate a state's own internal AI use, but would prevent states from regulating AI development or penalizing developers for third-party misuse of their models. Beyond preemption, the Framework calls for age-verification requirements for AI services accessible to children, streamlined federal permitting for AI infrastructure, protections for AI-generated digital replicas of individuals, and regulatory sandboxes to support innovation. While not itself legally binding, the Framework is expected to heavily shape federal AI legislation in the months ahead and could render several existing state AI laws unenforceable if broadly adopted.
